full_response:
Let K be the King’s (ground) frame and K′ be the rest–frame of Knight #2.  
Choose the x–axis of K along the race track and place the finish line at the spatial origin of K, x = 0.   
Two identical ground rods of length ℓ0 are laid end-to-end so that

 • the left–hand rod occupies −ℓ0 ≤ x ≤ 0  (start for Knight #2 at x = −ℓ0),  
 • the right–hand rod occupies 0 ≤ x ≤ +ℓ0  (start for Knight #1 at x = +ℓ0).

At t = 0 in K the knights are released, each with speed v = βc relative to the King.  
Knight #2 therefore moves in the +x–direction and at the instant of release is at the
start-line x = −ℓ0.  
(The finish line, x = 0, is between the two starting lines.)

Event E ≡ “Knight #2 crosses his own starting line”  
has K–frame coordinates

 tE = 0 , xE = − ℓ0 .

Because K′ moves with the knight at velocity +v, the standard Lorentz transformation
(from K to K′, with c = 1 in intermediate steps) is  

 t′ = γ ( t − βx ) ,  x′ = γ ( x − vt ) ,  γ ≡ 1/√{1 − β²} .

Insert the K–coordinates of event E:

 t2 ≡ t′E = γ [ 0 − β ( −ℓ0 ) ] = γ β ℓ0 /c ,  

 x2 ≡ x′E = γ [ ( −ℓ0 ) − v·0 ] = − γ ℓ0 .

Thus, in Knight #2’s own frame the coincidence with his starting line occurs

 at the time t2 = (γ β ℓ0 ) / c , and at the position x2 = − γ ℓ0 .

(The negative x2 simply means that, in his frame, the chosen origin— which is instantaneously
coincident with the finish line—lies a distance γℓ0 ahead of him.)

\boxed{\,t_2 = \dfrac{\gamma \beta \,\ell_0}{c},\qquad x_2 = -\,\gamma \,\ell_0\,}

Confidence: 45%
